stem: Which of the following is a prime number?
solutionStandard: 1 is not prime by definition. $9=3^2$, $21=3\times7$, and $27=3^3$ are all composite. $23$ has no divisors other than 1 and itself.
If $n=1$: $n^2=1$, $n+1=2$, so B is greater. If $n=3$: $n^2=9$, $n+1=4$, so A is greater. Since the relationship changes, it cannot be determined.
solutionShortcut: Plug in two different odd values (e.g. 1 and 3, or a negative odd value like -1) to see the relationship flips — this alone proves the answer is D.

stem: What is the greatest common divisor of 48 and 60?
solutionStandard: $48 = 2^4 \times 3$ and $60 = 2^2 \times 3 \times 5$. The GCD takes the lowest power of each shared prime: $2^2 \times 3 = 12$.

stem: What is the least common multiple of 8 and 12?
solutionStandard: $8 = 2^3$ and $12 = 2^2 \times 3$. The LCM takes the highest power of every prime present: $2^3 \times 3 = 24$.

stem: A number is divisible by 6. Which of the following must also be true? Select all that apply.
solutionStandard: $6 = 2 \times 3$, so any multiple of 6 is automatically a multiple of 2 and of 3, and every multiple of 2 is even. Divisibility by 12 is not guaranteed (e.g. 18 is divisible by 6 but not 12).

stem: For any integer $n$, which of the following must be even?
solutionStandard: $n^2 + n = n(n+1)$, the product of two consecutive integers. One of any two consecutive integers is always even, so the product is always even. (B), (D), and (E) depend on whether $n$ is even or odd, and (C) is always odd.
solutionShortcut: Plug in one even and one odd value of $n$ (e.g. $n=2$ and $n=3$) and eliminate any option that isn't even both times.

stem: What is the greatest number of consecutive integers, starting from 1, that can be multiplied together before the product exceeds 200?
solutionStandard: $1\times2\times3\times4=24$, and $1\times2\times3\times4\times5=120 \le 200$, but $\times 6 = 720 > 200$. So 5 integers (through 5!) is the greatest that keeps the product at or below 200.

stem: How many positive divisors does 60 have?
$p$ and $q$ are distinct prime numbers, both greater than 2.
solutionStandard: Every prime greater than 2 is odd, so $p+q$ is always even, but its size relative to 10 is not fixed: $p=3, q=5$ gives $p+q=8 < 10$; $p=3, q=7$ gives $p+q=10 = 10$; $p=5, q=7$ gives $p+q=12 > 10$. Since the relationship changes, it cannot be determined.

stem: $N$ is the smallest positive integer that is divisible by both 18 and 24. What is the sum of the digits of $N$?
